questions.question: What does the duty of candour expect of you and your organisation?
options: To be open and honest with people and those close to them at all times
To keep records private from the people who use the service
To speak openly only with other staff, never with families
To share information only when a formal complaint is made

questions.question: Why does being open with a person after a mistake matter so much?
options: It protects the organisation from ever having to change how it works
It is only worth doing if the person is likely to find out anyway
It matters only for very serious incidents and not for smaller ones
It keeps their trust, lets them get any follow-up care they need, and helps the team learn and prevent a repeat

questions.question: According to the course, what does hiding a mistake almost always do?
options: Has no real effect as long as no one finds out
Makes matters worse and stops the organisation from learning
Protects the person from unnecessary worry
Helps the whole team improve their practice

questions.question: In which piece of law is the duty of candour written for England?
options: Regulation 20 of the Health and Social Care Act 2008 (Regulated Activities) Regulations 2014
The Mental Capacity Act 2005
The Data Protection Act 2018
Section 20 of the Equality Act 2010

questions.question: Which body enforces the statutory duty of candour in England?
options: The General Medical Council
The Health and Safety Executive
The Nursing and Midwifery Council
The Care Quality Commission (CQC)

questions.question: Who does the statutory duty of candour in Regulation 20 apply to?
options: Nobody, because it is only good-practice guidance
The registered provider — the organisation and its registered manager
Only the person's GP or district nurse
Each individual care worker, who must personally notify the CQC

questions.question: Why is the statutory duty attached to the registered provider rather than to individual workers?
options: Because individuals cannot legally speak to families
Because care workers are not trusted to be honest
Because the standard is attached to CQC registration, which the organisation holds
Because only managers ever witness incidents

questions.question: What triggers the formal part of the statutory duty under Regulation 20?
options: A notifiable safety incident
Any complaint written by a family member
The annual CQC inspection visit
A staff member requesting supervision

questions.question: Which of these best describes a notifiable safety incident?
options: A disagreement between two members of staff
An unintended or unexpected incident during care that appears to have caused, or could cause, a significant degree of harm
A planned change to a person's care plan
Any minor error that is caught before it reaches the person

questions.question: Who judges whether an incident appears to have resulted in a significant degree of harm?
options: The CQC inspector during a routine visit
A healthcare professional, in their reasonable opinion
The person's family, on their own
The care worker who made the mistake, alone

questions.question: In the harm threshold, what does 'prolonged' pain or psychological harm mean?
options: Any harm that the person mentions more than once
Lasting for at least 24 hours
Lasting for exactly one week
Lasting, or likely to last, for a continuous period of at least 28 days

questions.question: What should you do rather than decide alone whether an incident meets the legal threshold?
options: Report clearly and promptly so your manager and healthcare professionals can assess it
Wait until the person complains before saying anything
Record it privately and take no further action
Decide yourself and only escalate if you are unsure

questions.question: Under Regulation 20, how must the provider first notify the person of a notifiable safety incident?
options: By waiting until the person asks what happened
In person, as soon as reasonably practicable, with a true account and an apology
By posting a general notice for all service users to read
Only in writing, several weeks after all enquiries are complete

questions.question: After the initial notification, what does Regulation 20 require the provider to do?
options: Ask the person to sign a form waiving any concerns
Close the matter with no further contact
Report the worker involved to the police
Follow up in writing, confirming the account and results of any enquiries, and keep a record

questions.question: Why is your honest, prompt report the foundation of the provider's formal duty?
options: The CQC requires each worker to notify it separately
The provider is not allowed to speak to families directly
Only written reports from workers count in law
The provider cannot notify anyone about an incident it does not know about

questions.question: When telling a person what has happened, how should you communicate?
options: Only in writing, to avoid a difficult conversation
Using technical terms so the news sounds less serious
Vaguely, so the person does not become upset
Directly and kindly, telling the truth plainly without hiding behind jargon

questions.question: How soon should a person be told after an incident comes to light?
options: As soon as is reasonably practicable
At the next scheduled review, whenever that falls
Only after every fact has been confirmed
Only if and when the person asks

questions.question: Being timely with candour means that you must:
options: Delay until managers have agreed a single version of events
Have every answer ready before you speak to the person
Wait to see whether the person noticed anything first
Say what you know now and make clear that more will follow as things become clearer

questions.question: You are giving an honest account but do not yet know why the incident happened. What should you do?
options: Use technical language so the explanation sounds less serious
Guess at a likely cause so the person has an answer straight away
Say nothing until every fact is confirmed, however long that takes
Explain what is known now, say what is not yet known, and describe what will be done to find out

questions.question: When giving a truthful account, you should never:
options: Use plain language free of jargon
Guess, speculate about blame, or shade the truth to make things sound better
Admit that the full picture is not yet understood
Describe what is known clearly and honestly

questions.question: What is true about an apology in England and Wales?
options: It is not, in itself, an admission of legal liability
It automatically proves the worker is legally at fault
It must always be approved by the CQC beforehand
It should only be given once enquiries are complete

questions.question: What makes an apology after a mistake meaningful?
options: It is written rather than spoken so it cannot be misquoted
It is sincere, specific about what happened, and shows genuine care for how the person was affected
It is delayed until the person raises the matter
It uses a careful form of words that avoids any responsibility

questions.question: What does the written follow-up required by Regulation 20 give the person?
options: A legal contract limiting the organisation's responsibility
A general newsletter about the service
A written account of the incident, details and results of enquiries, and a further apology
A bill for any additional care that was needed

questions.question: What is your part in the follow-up after an incident?
options: Leave all records to the manager and move on
Avoid writing anything down in case it is used against you
Record what happened and what was said accurately and promptly, and do what you said you would do
Change your account to match whatever the family expects

questions.question: Which regulators set out the professional duty of candour in joint guidance?
options: Local councils and the police
The CQC and the Health and Safety Executive
The Information Commissioner's Office and the CQC
The Nursing and Midwifery Council and the General Medical Council

questions.question: Being honest when something goes wrong is part of everyone's duty of candour, not only the manager's.
options: True
False

questions.question: The statutory duty of candour applies to individual care workers, who must each personally notify the CQC of incidents.
options: True
False

questions.question: Regulation 20 is a legal requirement, not just good-practice guidance.
options: True
False

questions.question: Every mistake is a notifiable safety incident in the legal sense.
options: True
False

questions.question: Saying sorry to a person after a mistake is an admission of legal liability.
options: True
False

questions.question: Candour must be timely, telling the person as soon as is reasonably practicable rather than only if they ask.
options: True
False

questions.question: Near misses do not need to be reported because no one was actually harmed.
options: True
False

questions.question: The law protects workers who raise genuine concerns in the public interest, a protection often called whistleblowing.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of these are reasons openness matters after something goes wrong? (Select all that apply)
options: It removes the need to keep any records
Honesty protects the relationship of trust between you and the person
It guarantees no one will ever be held accountable
People have a right to know what happened to them
Openness lets the whole team put things right and prevent a repeat

questions.question: According to the course, what is your part in candour as an individual worker? (Select all that apply)
options: Decide alone whether the incident is legally notifiable
Be honest and never hide or minimise a mistake
Report what has happened promptly to your senior or manager
Make the formal notification to the CQC yourself
Support the person, reassure them and see they are cared for

questions.question: Which features describe a notifiable safety incident? (Select all that apply)
options: The harm reaches a defined threshold judged by a healthcare professional
It is unintended or unexpected
It is any minor error, regardless of harm
It was a planned and normal part of the person's care
It happened during the provision of the care service

questions.question: Which steps must the provider take under Regulation 20 when a notifiable safety incident happens? (Select all that apply)
options: Notify the person, or their representative, in person as soon as reasonably practicable
Post a general notice for all service users to read
Give a true account of what is known and offer an apology
Follow up in writing and keep a record
Wait until the person makes a formal complaint

questions.question: Which of these are part of giving a truthful account? (Select all that apply)
options: Not minimising or playing down the seriousness
Using vague phrases to soften the truth
Guessing at blame to give the person a quick answer
Not speculating about causes before enquiries are done
Sticking to the facts as they are known

questions.question: What does the professional duty of candour require of every worker? (Select all that apply)
options: Be open with people and tell them when something has gone wrong
Be open and honest with colleagues and employers
Keep near misses to yourself unless a colleague also saw them
Support the person and offer help to put things right
Only report mistakes that cause serious harm

questions.question: Which statements describe a just and learning culture? (Select all that apply)
options: Incidents are used to improve care for everyone
Nobody is ever held accountable for anything they do
Mistakes are kept quiet so the organisation looks good
People are treated fairly when they are open about honest mistakes
Workers feel safe to raise concerns without fear

questions.question: Saying sorry is not, in itself, an admission of legal ___.
In England the duty of candour is set out in ___ 20, enforced by the CQC.
A ___ miss is when something almost went wrong but was caught before it reached the person.
For 'prolonged' harm, the harm lasts, or is likely to last, for a continuous period of at least ___ days.
Candour thrives in a just and ___ culture, where people feel safe to be honest about mistakes.
